ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for tomato beef rice noodle dish specifically; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers rice noodles as a base ingredient category; no composite dish code exists for tomato beef rice noodle soup in FoodEx2. Mapped to rice noodle base as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for this composite noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of tomato beef slices rice noodles as served in a Singapore Chinese noodle restaurant typically weighs approximately 450 g, comprising rice noodles, thinly sliced fatty beef, tomato-based broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Tomato Beef Slices Rice Noodles 番茄肥牛小锅米线
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tomato Beef Slices Rice Noodles (番茄肥牛小锅米线), cr_id G00010509, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and rice noodle component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (fresh/boiled, GI ~61, medium category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 450 g serving / 100) = 61 × 45.3 / 100 ≈ 27.6. The presence of beef protein and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) is expected to modestly attenuate the glycaemic response in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth and soy sauce to limit sodium intake, while the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and dietary fibre content provide partial DASH-diet alignment; a standard 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1040 mg sodium, which represents 45–52% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles have medium GI (~61); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g and beef protein moderate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard bowl and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycaemic load; the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and beef protein slow gastric emptying, but a full 450 g serving delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 27.6, which is high and requires insulin or medication adjustment awareness.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fatty beef (fei niu) contributes saturated fat; sodium load from broth; beneficial potassium and fibre present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption due to the saturated fat content from fatty beef slices and the sodium load from broth; substituting lean beef or tofu and requesting low-sodium broth can reduce cardiovascular risk while preserving the nutritional benefits of potassium (327 mg/100g), dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), and iron (2.55 mg/100g).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fatty beef (fei niu) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); tomato is a nightshade that may trigger joint sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of fatty beef slices and the potential nightshade sensitivity from tomato; substituting with chicken, tofu, or shrimp and requesting tomato-free broth is recommended to reduce TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathway activation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Beef Slices Rice Noodles (番茄肥牛小锅米线, fānqié féi niú xiǎo guō mǐ xiàn) is a popular Chinese noodle dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and food courts. The dish consists of smooth, silky rice vermicelli noodles submerged in a tangy, savoury tomato-based broth, topped with thinly sliced fatty beef (肥牛, fei niu) that cooks gently in the hot broth, along with garnishes such as spring onion and sometimes leafy vegetables. The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce, giving it a rich umami depth balanced by the natural acidity and sweetness of tomatoes. The texture profile combines the soft, slippery noodles with tender, melt-in-the-mouth beef slices and a warming, slightly acidic soup. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The medium glycaemic index (~61) from rice noodles is moderated by the dietary fibre and beef protein content. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires sodium monitoring for hypertension patients, portion control for type 2 diabetes management due to its medium-to-high glycaemic load, and avoidance or modification for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory fatty beef and nightshade tomato components. The dish is a staple lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010509, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and rice noodle component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle analogue. All mandatory multilingual names provided.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010509, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. Saturated_fat_g of 1.6 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked beef (chuck, fatty) and rice noodles weighted by approximate dish composition; this is an adapted estimate and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. Cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is plausible given the dish composition where beef slices contribute minimal cholesterol at the reported serving ratio — confirmed zero per HPB source. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle entry; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish has been published. Halal status is marked false as fei niu (fatty beef) sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-specific verification.
